BakeryMutex: Lamport's bakery doesn't depend on any atomic primitive richer than read and write of single words, and that historical fact dictates the entire variable layout. Three pieces of state per process are unavoidable: a program counter to distinguish ncs/choose/wait/cs phases, a number ticket carrying the priority, and a choosing flag that exists solely to plug the race window between reading the maximum and committing to a ticket. Without choosing[j], a process i could see number[j]=0 and walk past it just as j was about to assign itself a number larger than i's. The Enter action enforces the lexicographic LessEq comparison on (number[i], i), which is the heart of the algorithm: ties on the natural number are resolved deterministically by process id, so two processes can never simultaneously satisfy their respective enter guards. Mutual exclusion is therefore inductive on Enter alone -- the other actions touch number and choosing in ways that only widen the wait, never the entry condition. The MaxNum constraint and the Idle self-loop together keep the state space finite without distorting the protocol: real bakery has unbounded tickets, but bounding by 3 only restricts when Pick can fire, not how Enter resolves ties.
Barrier: An N-thread barrier is best understood as two disjoint sets of processes -- those still gathering and those already released -- with a single atomic flip between them once the gathering set fills. Modeling this with arrived and past as subsets of Procs (rather than counters) makes the safety property direct: the cross-set BarrierSafe says past is either empty or holds exactly N members and the two sets never overlap, which is precisely the guarantee a barrier user expects. Arrive(p) only adds to arrived and only when p hasn't been there this round; Release fires atomically when arrived has filled to N, sweeping every process into past in a single transition; Reset clears past for the next iteration. The atomic Release is what makes the safety inductive -- there is no intermediate state where some processes have crossed and others haven't, so a witness to past being a strict non-empty subset of Procs simply cannot arise. Bounding the state space comes for free from |Procs| being a constant CONSTANT, since both subsets live in the finite powerset.
BatchAdmission: Rate limiting in fixed buckets is fundamentally about a counter that monotonically rises within an epoch and discontinuously resets at epoch boundaries. The two variables this_tick and total separate those concerns: this_tick is the per-bucket admitted count that the safety property must constrain, while total is a wrap-around counter mod 2B+1 that exists to keep the state space finite without making admit actions trivially commutative. The Admit guard this_tick < B is the only place mutual exclusion of safety lives -- once the counter equals B, no further Admit is enabled until TickBoundary resets it to zero. TickBoundary is unconditional, modeling that real time may advance at any moment; this is the right abstraction because admission control must be safe regardless of when the bucket rolls. The invariant BatchInv merely asserts that this_tick stays within 0..B and total within its modular range, which is preserved by both actions: Admit's guard prevents overshoot, and TickBoundary lands exactly on zero.
BoundedQueue: Capacity-limited FIFO is the canonical use case for a TLA+ sequence variable, because Seq already gives ordering and length for free without auxiliary indexing. A single variable queue of type Seq(Vals) is necessary and sufficient: any extra head/tail indices would be redundant given that Append and Tail provide constant-time insertion at the back and removal from the front. Enqueue(v) is guarded by Len(queue) < K, which is the bound that makes Bounded inductive -- no other action can grow the sequence -- and Dequeue is guarded by Len(queue) > 0, which is the bound that prevents underflow. FIFO order is not stated as a separate property because it is structurally enforced: Tail removes the head and Append adds at the tail, so the i-th element to enter is necessarily the i-th to leave. The state space is bounded by |Vals|^K + |Vals|^(K-1) + ... + 1, which is finite as long as both K and Vals are finite constants.
BoundedStack: A LIFO stack and a FIFO queue look identical at the type level -- both are bounded sequences of values -- yet the semantic difference is concentrated in a single line: Pop calls SubSeq(stack, 1, Len(stack) - 1) instead of Tail. That choice removes the most recently appended element and is what makes the data structure last-in-first-out. Push uses Append, the same primitive as a queue, but combined with this Pop the result is that pop returns whatever value the most recent push placed at the top. The bounded invariant is identical to the queue case (length in 0..K, all elements drawn from Vals) because boundedness is a structural property of the carrier, not the discipline. The Push guard Len(stack) < K prevents overflow and is the only action that grows the sequence; the Pop guard Len(stack) > 0 prevents removing from an empty stack. No order-tracking variable is needed because the sequence itself records insertion order.
BurnsMutex: Burns' algorithm is interesting because it uses only a single binary flag per process and exploits a strict process-id priority ordering -- lower-id processes always have precedence. The variable layout reflects this: pc tracks the six phases each process cycles through, and flag is a per-process boolean that is set only when the process intends to enter and has confirmed no lower-id competitor is contending. The entry protocol is cautious-then-aggressive: ClearAndCheck resets the flag and looks at lower priorities; if any are competing, the process restarts; if not, SetFlag raises the flag and RecheckLow re-validates that no lower-id process raced in during the gap. The double-check is what prevents a higher-priority latecomer from being missed. WaitHigh then blocks until no higher-id process holds its flag, which is the symmetric condition for a higher-id process to enter behind us. The CS guard reduces to: at the moment of entry, no other process has flag set in a way that would beat me. Mutual exclusion is inductive because two processes cannot simultaneously satisfy WaitHigh -- whichever has the lower id is checked first by the higher.
CausalBroadcast: Causal delivery is a constraint on the order in which messages are presented to the application, not on the order they arrive on the wire, so the spec must distinguish in-flight from delivered. The vector clock vc[p][q] counts how many messages from sender q have been delivered at p, and the channel is a set of in-flight tagged messages where each tag is a snapshot of the sender's vc at the moment of broadcast plus one increment for the sender's own slot. DeliverRemote enforces the two classical causal predicates: the message's slot for its own sender must be exactly one more than the receiver's current count for that sender (no skipping a sender's prior message), and every other slot must already be matched at the receiver (no delivering a message whose causes haven't arrived). DeliverSelf is separate because the sender must reflect its own broadcast in its own vc immediately, otherwise its later messages would carry inconsistent timestamps. The TypeOK conjuncts vc[p][q] <= sentCount[q] capture the honesty of vector counts: a process cannot have observed more messages from q than q has actually sent, and the channel never carries phantom counts.
ChangRoberts: On a unidirectional ring, the cleverness of Chang-Roberts is that no node knows the global topology -- a node simply forwards a token only if the token's id strictly exceeds its own, and otherwise drops it. This suppression rule guarantees that only the maximum id ever survives a full lap, and the surviving token is recognized by the predicate val = dest the moment it lands on its originator. The state is a multiset of in-flight messages plus a single leader cell that is 0 until election concludes. Forward(m) re-injects m at the successor with the same val, Drop(m) removes any token whose val is less than its current dest position (or any leftover token after election), and Elect(m) fires only when val equals dest, recording that node as leader. The Reset action exists purely to keep the model live: once the unique token has been consumed and the leader is recorded, the protocol re-seeds itself for another round, preventing terminal deadlock without distorting the safety claim. SafetyInv -- if a leader exists, no process has a strictly larger id -- is inductive because Elect can only fire on a token equal to the global maximum (any smaller token would have been suppressed earlier in its lap).
CigaretteSmokers: Patil's puzzle is a coordination problem dressed up as scarcity: each smoker permanently owns one ingredient, the agent provides exactly the missing two, and the smoker who matches the table's contents is the unique one who can proceed. Modeling the table as a set of ingredients (rather than a multiset or sequence) is the key abstraction because the puzzle requires only presence, not count. The companion variable smoking holds either NoSmoker or the index of the actively-smoking party, so the spec captures both phases of a smoker's life cycle in two state variables. AgentPut guards on table = {} and smoking = NoSmoker, ensuring the agent never refills mid-cycle; StartSmoke(s) requires that s's lacking pair is a subset of the table and atomically removes those two ingredients while marking s as smoking; FinishSmoke clears smoking. The safety predicate SmokerInv encodes the puzzle's contract: while smoking, the table is empty -- because the smoker has consumed exactly the two ingredients it needed -- so two smokers cannot simultaneously be smoking from the same agent put.
CircuitBreaker: The defining property of a circuit breaker is that the half-open state admits at most one probe at a time, and that single bottleneck is what makes the spec's three-variable layout -- cbState, failures, probeInFlight -- both necessary and sufficient. cbState is the main mode, failures is the consecutive-failure tally that only matters in CLOSED, and probeInFlight is a boolean gate that turns half-open into a one-shot region. ClosedFailure increments failures and trips OPEN exactly when the count reaches FailLimit, snapping failures back to zero so the invariant (cbState = closed) => failures < FailLimit holds. TimeoutToHalfOpen is the only path out of OPEN, and StartProbe is guarded on probeInFlight = FALSE, so the half-open phase serializes probes through a binary mutex. ProbeSuccess and ProbeFailure both clear probeInFlight, but they branch on the new mode: success closes the breaker, failure re-opens. The TypeOK conjuncts (probeInFlight = TRUE) => (cbState = half_open) and the failure-zero clauses for OPEN and HALF_OPEN are what make the safety inductive -- they pin probeInFlight to its only meaningful context and prevent failures from leaking across mode transitions.
CircularBuffer: Multi-producer/multi-consumer ring buffers are usually implemented with two monotone counters rather than head/tail pointers into a fixed array, because monotone counters never need wrap-around reasoning and the difference tail - head directly gives the live size. This spec adopts that abstraction wholesale: head and tail are natural numbers, the live size is tail - head, and the K capacity bound is enforced as tail - head < K on Produce. Consume's guard head < tail is the dual condition that prevents underflow. The MaxOps cap is pure model-checking discipline -- it bounds tail (and transitively head, since head <= tail) so the state space is finite, but it does not affect the relative invariant tail - head <= K which is what callers actually rely on. WF_vars(Next) is the only liveness assumption: with weak fairness, both Produce and Consume eventually fire when enabled, so the buffer doesn't stall at any constant occupancy. The Bounded predicate folds the size and ordering constraints together, and inductiveness is straightforward -- each action changes exactly one counter by one and the guards ensure the tail >= head and tail - head <= K facets are preserved.
ConsistentHashRing: Consistent hashing's appeal is that adding or removing a node only redistributes the keys whose successor on the ring changes, leaving all other keys exactly where they were. To capture that property abstractly, the spec only needs to track which nodes are alive -- node hash positions are deterministic functions of the node id, and the routing for any key is then a closed-form CHOOSE over the alive set. The single state variable alive is a subset of NodeIds, and the EveryKeyMapped invariant is the load-bearing safety claim: every key in the universe maps to some alive node. Fail(n) is guarded by Cardinality(alive) > 1, preventing the degenerate empty-ring state where Successor would have nothing to choose; Recover(n) restores a previously failed node. The Successor function handles the wrap-around case explicitly -- if no alive node has hash >= p it returns the alive node with the smallest hash, which is the standard ring-traversal semantics. Inductiveness of EveryKeyMapped follows from the fact that as long as alive is non-empty, Successor always returns a member of alive, so Owner(k) cannot become orphaned no matter which node fails.
CoordinatorRecovery: Two-phase commit with coordinator recovery has a single subtle invariant: once any decision is recorded, no subsequent action -- including a backup takeover -- may overwrite it. The variable layout makes this explicit by giving the global decision its own scalar that is initialized to none and only assigned by PrimaryDecide or BackupDecide, with both guarded on decision = none. That guard is the heart of uniform agreement: the backup is structurally incapable of revisiting a decision the primary already made before crashing. Each participant additionally has a pState (working/voted/commit/abort) and pVote, so the per-participant decision is encoded by transitioning pState via the Learn(p) action which copies the global decision into the local state. The primary/backup roles are tracked by two enum variables that gate which side may decide: BackupTakeover only fires after primary = crashed, and BackupDecide only fires while backup = active and decision = none. SafetyInv -- every participant in a committed/aborted state matches the global decision -- is inductive because the only action that can change pState into a Decisions value is Learn, and Learn copies decision atomically.
CountingSemaphore: A counting semaphore over capacity K invites a deceptively simple two-variable layout: an integer count and a set of holders. The trick is that these two are not independent -- they are linked by a conservation law, count + |holders| = K, that the spec elevates from a derived fact to part of the inductive invariant. Acquire(p) decrements count and adds p to holders; both updates happen atomically in the same action, so conservation is preserved at every step. Release(p) is guarded on p \in holders, which forbids unauthorized releases that would otherwise corrupt the count; it then atomically increments count and removes p. The guards count > 0 on Acquire and count < K on Release are not redundant with the conservation law -- they are exactly what the law degenerates to, but stating both makes the actions self-contained. Conservation is the strong invariant; bounding count to 0..K is the type discipline that makes it checkable. The state space is finite because |holders| is bounded by |Procs| and count by K.
CpuQuota: CPU quota enforcement over a periodic window is the canonical use case for a usage map plus a clock: per-task counters that monotonically rise within a period, plus a single shared clock that ticks until it hits the period boundary, at which point both the clock and every task's usage reset together. The spec gives each task its own slot in used and a single global clock variable, which is sufficient because tasks compete only against the shared time bound and their own quota -- there is no inter-task interaction beyond the implicit serialization of the Run action over different t's. Run(t) requires both used[t] < Q (the per-task quota) and clock < P (the period not yet elapsed), so neither bound can be exceeded. Idle advances the clock without consuming any task's quota, modeling slack. Reset is guarded on clock = P and atomically zeroes used and clock, which is the only place quota gets returned. QuotaInv asserting used[t] in 0..Q is inductive because Run's guard prevents overshoot and Reset is the only thing that decreases used, always to zero.
CsmaCd: Carrier sense with collision detection is naturally modeled as a per-station mode plus a shared channel that records who is currently asserting carrier. The channel is a set of stations because the protocol's safety question is set-theoretic: how many transmitters are simultaneously asserting? More than one means collision. The TypeOK conjunct channel = { s : mode[s] = tx } makes the channel a derived view of the per-station modes, and elevating that equality to an invariant means the spec can refer to either side interchangeably. Sense moves a station from idle to sensing without touching the channel; BeginTx fires only when channel is empty, atomically setting the station to tx and asserting carrier; SenseBusy is the alternative path where the channel is non-empty and the station retreats to backoff. DetectCollision fires precisely when |channel| >= 2 -- the colliding station withdraws from the channel and enters collide. FinishTx requires channel = {s}, the lone-transmitter condition, and only then does the station release the channel cleanly. The cycle through collide/backoff/retry is what enforces eventual progress without violating safety.
DeadlineScheduler: Earliest-deadline-first scheduling has a single invariant worth proving: at every moment the running task has the smallest remaining deadline among ready tasks. To make that property a state predicate, the spec stores per-task deadlines in dl, the set of ready tasks in ready, and the currently running task (or NoTask) in running. Dispatch picks any ready task whose deadline equals the current minimum, which is computed by a CHOOSE over the image of dl on ready -- this is where the EDF policy is enforced and where the EdfInv invariant becomes inductive. Tick decreases every task's remaining deadline together, including the running task's, so the relative ordering across ready tasks is preserved; Complete refreshes the running task's deadline to MaxDL and returns it to the ready set. Because aging is uniform, EdfInv survives ticks: if the running task had the smallest dl before, it still does after a uniform decrement (clamped at zero only spreads ties, never reorders strictly). The bounded MaxDL keeps the state space finite without affecting the invariant's inductiveness.
DeadlockDetector: A wait-for graph deadlock detector is doubly interesting because the safety property is a global graph property -- no cycle anywhere -- yet must be checkable as a state predicate over a small finite model. The spec keeps things tractable by storing waitsFor as a function from each process to either NoOne or another process, so the wait-for graph is a functional graph with at most one outgoing edge per node. Cycles in such a graph have length at most N, and the recursive Reaches operator unrolls reachability up to depth N, which is sufficient to detect any cycle. StartWait(p, q) refuses to fire if Reaches(q, p, N) is already true, structurally preventing cycles from forming -- the spec models a lock manager that has correct cycle prevention and asks the detector merely to certify the absence. The alarm variable is therefore vacuously false in every reachable state, and NoCycleInv is the strong safety claim. The inductiveness rests entirely on StartWait's pre-check: any new edge that would close a cycle is rejected, so cycles cannot accumulate.
DekkerMutex: Dekker's two-process mutex is a careful dance between flag (announce intent) and turn (break ties), and the program counter must enumerate every observation point at which a process can be preempted, which is why pc has five values rather than the usual three. Raise sets flag[i] and moves to check; CheckOther branches: if the other's flag is clear, enter directly; otherwise consult turn. Yield is the back-off path -- when it isn't this process's turn, it lowers its own flag and waits, which is the asymmetric step that makes deadlock impossible (one process always defers to whichever turn currently points to). Persist is the symmetric case where consulting turn finds it pointing here, in which case the process retains its raised flag and re-checks the other. TakeTurn re-raises the flag after the wait, and Leave passes turn to the other process on exit. Mutual exclusion is inductive because the only path to cs runs through CheckOther observing flag[Other(i)] = FALSE; that observation, combined with the turn-passing in Leave, is what guarantees the other process cannot have simultaneously taken its own cs path.
DhcpLease: DORA -- discover, offer, request, ack -- is a four-message handshake whose safety contract reduces to one statement: at most one client may hold any given address simultaneously. The spec encodes the server's state with two parallel maps over Addrs (status and owner) and the client side with a per-client cstate, plus an explicit msgs set holding in-flight protocol messages. Modeling messages as a set rather than a sequence reflects the broadcast nature of DHCP. Each phase consumes one message and produces the next: Discover emits a discover token, Offer consumes it and atomically marks the address as offered with that client as owner, Request advances the client to requesting, and Ack flips status from offered to leased. The crucial safety conjunct (a # b /\ status[a] # free /\ status[b] # free) => owner[a] # owner[b] is the no-double-allocation rule, made inductive by Offer's guard status[a] = free which prevents a second client from being offered an already-claimed address. The owner=NoOwner iff status=free conjunct keeps the two server maps in lockstep.
DijkstraMutex: Dijkstra's 1965 algorithm is the first software solution that scales to N processes, and its three shared variables -- b[], c[], k -- play distinct roles: b is the loosely-claimed busy flag, c is the tightly-claimed CS flag, and k is the process whose b currently has priority. The five-stage pc enumerates the protocol's observation points. Start clears b[i] to announce intent. L2_retreat handles the case k # i: the process restores c[i] to TRUE so it doesn't block others, and if k itself is no longer busy, claims k = i. L2_advance fires only once k = i, advancing to L3 where c[i] is cleared as the strong claim. L4 then checks that no other process has cleared its own c -- if so, enter the CS; if any other c[j] is FALSE, L4_fail backs off, restoring both b and c, and returns to L2 to retry. The mutual exclusion guarantee is inductive because two processes cannot simultaneously satisfy L4's guard -- the first to clear its c[j] is the one whose subsequent L4 sees the others still set, and the second's L4 fails on the first's cleared flag, forcing a retry.
DiningPhilosophers: The classic deadlock in dining philosophers comes from the cyclic wait that arises when every philosopher acquires forks in the same order around the table. Dijkstra's elegant fix is asymmetry: philosopher 0 picks up its higher-numbered fork first while everyone else picks up the lower-numbered fork first. That single inversion breaks the cycle in the wait-for graph. The spec captures this with FirstFork and SecondFork helpers that branch on i = 0, then runs a four-state lifecycle (thinking, hungry, oneFork, eating) per philosopher, plus a holds map saying which philosopher holds each fork (or NoOne). PickFirst and PickSecond each require the corresponding fork to be free, atomically claiming it. Release sweeps both forks back to NoOne in one step. NoAdjacentEatingInv is the directly-checkable safety property: philosopher i eating implies philosopher (i+1) mod N is not eating, which follows because they share a fork and a fork has a single holder. The boundedness is trivial: per-philosopher state is finite, holds is a function over a finite domain.
DirectoryCoherence: Directory-based MSI coherence is a careful invariant maintenance problem across two layers: the directory's bookkeeping (sharers and owner) and each cache's local state (I/S/M). The spec elevates the consistency of these layers to invariants, which is what makes the safety properties inductive rather than incidental. SharersAgreement says the directory's sharers set is exactly the caches in S; OwnerAgreement says the directory's owner field corresponds to the unique cache in M; OwnerExcludesShared says ownership and sharing are mutually exclusive at the global level. GetShared adds a cache to sharers when nothing is owned. GetSharedFromOwner is the writeback-on-read path: the owner is downgraded to S, the new reader joins as S, and the directory's owner clears. GetModified is the invalidation broadcast: every other cache is forced to I and the writer takes ownership, sweeping sharers to empty in the same action. Eviction paths return the cache to I and update the directory accordingly. SingleOwner -- at most one M cache -- is the headline safety, and it is preserved by GetModified being the only writer-creating action and by the atomic invalidation it performs.
DmaTransfer: A DMA engine's correctness reduces to a single contract: when the engine reports done, the destination memory slot really does hold the source value the descriptor recorded. Reifying that contract requires the spec to model memory itself as a state variable, plus a descriptor record holding src/dst/v fields, plus the engine's own state machine and an interrupt line. Issue programs the descriptor and demands that the recorded v actually equals memory[s] at issue time -- this nails down the descriptor's source value to a concrete memory contents and is what makes the eventual completion check meaningful. Complete is where the state-altering work happens: memory[desc.dst] is updated to desc.v, the engine moves to done, and the interrupt line goes high, all atomically. Acknowledge clears the interrupt and returns to idle. The two strong invariants -- DoneImpliesCopied and InterruptIffDone -- cover both the data contract and the signaling contract, and both are inductive because Complete is the unique action that establishes them and Acknowledge is the unique action that clears the interrupt without touching memory.
Dolev: Dolev-Strong's central idea is that a value is only forwarded once it has accumulated f+1 distinct signatures, which guarantees that at least one honest node has signed it; therefore any honest receiver who collects f+1 signatures has received corroborating attestation from at least one honest peer in every round. The spec captures the round structure with an explicit round counter advancing from 0 to MaxRound = f+1, and a sigs map from value to the set of nodes that have signed it. Broadcast(v) records the sender as the first signer and bumps the round to 1. Sign(n, v) lets each honest node add its signature in subsequent rounds, only if v is already non-empty (i.e., it has been forwarded into this round). Tick advances the round counter without changing signatures, modeling the synchronous boundary. Accept(n, v) fires only at MaxRound and only when sigs[v] >= f+1 and no other value has reached the threshold -- this exclusivity is what makes agreement inductive. SafetyInv asserts pairwise agreement among nodes that have accepted, and it holds because Accept's guard requires sigs[v] >= f+1 with no competitor at the same level, so two distinct accepted values would both need f+1 disjoint signers, which exceeds the honest population.
DoubleEndedQueue: A deque generalizes both stacks and queues: insertions and removals at both ends. The spec uses a single Seq(Vals) variable named deque, which is sufficient because TLA+'s sequence operators give symmetric access to both ends -- PushFront uses prepend (<<v>> \o deque), PushBack uses Append, PopFront uses Tail, and PopBack uses SubSeq up to Len-1. All four mutating actions share the same boundedness discipline: pushes are guarded by Len < K to prevent overflow and pops by Len > 0 to prevent underflow. The contract that PushFront followed by PopFront returns the same value is structural: prepending v makes deque[1] = v, and Tail removes deque[1], so any observer reading the head sees the just-pushed value. Symmetrically for PushBack and PopBack at index Len(deque). The Bounded invariant asserts both the length cap and the per-element membership in Vals; both are inductive because no action can exceed K (push guards) or introduce a non-Vals value (the Vals quantification on each push action).
EightQueensSmall: N-queens is a constraint-satisfaction problem traditionally solved by backtracking, but the spec models it as an incremental construction: the state is a sequence of row positions, one per filled column, and the only mutating action appends a new row only when it preserves non-attacking among all already-placed queens. Choosing a sequence indexed by column makes column-distinctness automatic -- each index appears at most once -- so the spec only needs to forbid same-row and same-diagonal attacks. NoAttack checks both via |row[i] - row[j]| comparisons against |i - j|. Place is guarded by NonAttacking(queens'), which is the canonical incremental constraint: every prefix is feasible. The Done self-loop fires when either the board is full or no row can be appended without a conflict, preventing terminal deadlock. SafetyInv requires NonAttacking(queens), and it is inductive because Place is the only action that can extend the sequence and it explicitly demands NonAttacking on the new sequence; the empty initial state trivially satisfies it.
EpochLeader: Epoch-based leadership tracks two pieces of information per node -- its current epoch -- plus two global facts: the highest epoch ever observed (maxSeen), and the elected leader for each epoch. The leader map is the safety-relevant variable: at most one entry per epoch slot is the desired property. Bump(n) advances n's own epoch only when n is already at maxSeen and the next slot exists; this serializes epoch advancement so that no two nodes can be at the latest epoch unless they took turns. BecomeLeader(n) fires only when n is at maxSeen and leader[epoch[n]] = 0, so the second guard is the per-epoch single-leader gate that makes safety inductive. Reset wipes the state once an epoch slot has a leader and maxSeen has reached its cap, keeping the model finite. SafetyInv (a recorded leader's epoch is at least the epoch slot it occupies) holds because BecomeLeader writes leader[epoch[n]] only with n's current epoch, so the leader's epoch field is necessarily exactly the slot index at the moment of election, and Bump only ever increases it afterward.
EventCount: Reed and Kanodia's event count is a synchronization primitive that stores a monotonically increasing integer and lets processes block until the integer reaches some threshold, providing a counted alternative to condition variables. The spec needs three pieces: count itself, a per-process pc to distinguish idle/waiting/done, and a per-process target recording the threshold each waiter is blocked on. StartAwait(i, v) snapshots the threshold; Advance is unconditional (modulo the bound) and bumps count, modeling either an internal event or an external advance call; Wake(i) fires only when count >= target[i], releasing the waiter into done. Reset cycles a finished waiter back to idle so the model loops. The TypeOK conjunct pc[i] = done => count >= target[i] is the load-bearing safety: it asserts that no process ever transitions to done unless its target was actually met. Inductiveness follows because Wake is the unique action that produces a done state, and Wake's guard is exactly the conjunct that TypeOK demands.
FairMutex: Fairness in a mutex is more than mutual exclusion -- it requires bounded bypass, the property that no waiter is overtaken indefinitely. The standard mechanism for that is a turn pointer cycling round-robin through process ids, and this spec realizes it directly with three variables: pc per process, the global turn pointer, and a lock cell holding either NoHolder or the current holder's id. EnterCS is guarded on turn = i AND lock = NoHolder, and Release atomically clears the lock and rotates turn to (turn % N) + 1, so successive critical sections necessarily go to successive ids. Skip is the safety valve: if turn points at a process still in ncs, the spec advances turn so the lock isn't blocked by an idle thread -- this is the critical clause that makes the protocol live, otherwise a single idle process could starve all others. The TypeOK conjunct (lock # NoHolder) => pc[lock] = cs links the two state pieces, ensuring lock can only name a process actually in its critical section. Mutual exclusion is enforced because EnterCS demands lock = NoHolder, and only Release clears it.
FairQueue: Per-flow fair queueing across multiple competing flows is a scheduling discipline whose correctness lies in the cursor's strict round-robin advance, regardless of which flow's queue is actually drained. The spec uses a per-flow occupancy count q[f] and a single shared cursor variable. Enqueue(f) just bumps q[f] up to QCap, leaving the cursor untouched, which models that producers don't perturb fairness. Dequeue is gated on q[cursor] > 0 -- it pops from the cursor's flow and rotates the cursor to (cursor + 1) % N. Skip is the structurally identical action when q[cursor] = 0: it advances the cursor without dequeuing anything. The two together guarantee that the cursor visits every flow in turn even when some are empty, which is the structural source of the no-starvation-in-window-of-N property. FairnessInv just asserts the cursor and per-flow counts stay within their finite ranges, and it is inductive because every action either preserves both bounds or moves them by exactly one within range.
FastMutex: Lamport's fast mutex is engineered for the uncontended case: in the absence of contention, the protocol completes in five memory operations and never spins. The spec faithfully models the seven-stage pc, including the two re-checks that distinguish fast-path entry from slow-path waiting. Start raises b[i]; SetX writes x = i; CheckY tests the second handshake variable -- if y is held by anyone, the process restarts, otherwise it proceeds to SetY which writes y = i. CheckX then re-reads x: if it still equals i, no other process intervened and the fast path commits; otherwise the slow path is taken via WaitB, which spins until every other b[j] is FALSE and then re-tests y = i to determine whether this process is the slow-path winner. The crucial point is that x and y serve dual roles -- x is the first claimant in line, and y is the latest claimant; only the process whose id appears in both is the unambiguous winner. Mutual exclusion is inductive because cs entry requires either CheckX seeing x = i (which means no other process raced into SetX) or WaitB seeing y = i with all other b[j] cleared (the slow-path serialization).
FastPaxos: Fast Paxos shaves a round trip off classic Paxos by letting clients submit proposals directly to acceptors, but pays for it with a larger fast quorum (3 out of 4 here) and a recovery path when collisions happen. The spec captures this with three variables: an accepted map per acceptor, a global chosen value, and a round mode (fast or classic). FastAccept lets any acceptor adopt any value while still in fast mode and not yet committed; FastChoose fires when a fast quorum agrees on a single value and writes chosen accordingly. FallBack triggers on a collision -- two acceptors holding distinct values -- and switches to classic, modeling the recovery round. ClassicChoose then takes any already-accepted value, overrides every acceptor with it, and sets chosen, modeling the coordinator's safe pick rule. SafetyInv -- chosen value is still backed by some fast quorum -- holds inductively because FastChoose only fires when the quorum already agrees, and ClassicChoose forcibly aligns every acceptor before assigning chosen. Reset cycles the state for the next instance.
FetchAndAddMutex: A ticket lock built from fetch-and-add has two counters that together impose a strict FIFO order: next_ticket is the dispenser that hands each arrival a unique id, and serving is the announcement of which ticket is currently allowed in. Per-process ticket[i] remembers the value each thread drew. Acquire(i) atomically copies next_ticket into ticket[i] and increments next_ticket -- modeling fetch-and-add as a single TLA+ action -- so two simultaneous Acquires will receive distinct numbers. EnterCS spins on ticket[i] = serving, and Release increments serving by one, handing the lock to whoever drew the next number. Mutual exclusion is inductive because two processes cannot both have ticket[i] = serving (their tickets are distinct integers and only one matches serving at a time), and FIFO ordering is structural: ticket[i] < ticket[j] implies i is served strictly before j. The MaxTicket bound is purely a state-space cap.
FifoCache: A FIFO cache holds at most K distinct keys in insertion order, evicting the oldest when a new key arrives at full capacity. Modeling this as a sequence rather than a set is essential because eviction policy depends on insertion order, which a set discards. The cache variable is Seq(Keys) with the head being the oldest entry. Insert(k) requires k absent (no duplicates) and branches on length: if there is room, append; otherwise drop the head with Tail and append the new key in one action, capturing eviction-then-insert atomically. Remove(k) handles arbitrary explicit removal by splitting the sequence at the located index. The Distinct invariant asserts no duplicates -- which Insert preserves because of its absence guard, and Remove preserves because removing one occurrence of a unique element leaves uniqueness intact. The bounded length stays within 0..K because the only growth path (Insert with room) checks Len < K, and the eviction-on-full path keeps the length exactly at K.
FifteenPuzzle: The 8-puzzle is a sliding-tile permutation problem, and the spec captures it as a function from cell coordinates to tile values 0..8 plus an explicit blank position. Storing the blank separately is a key efficiency: every Slide action only needs to know which tiles are adjacent to the blank, and Adj(blank, c) tests for orthogonal neighbors. Slide swaps the blank's value (zero) with the chosen neighbor's tile and updates the blank position to the neighbor in a single atomic step, which is the right level of abstraction because real sliding is one motion. The strong invariant SafetyInv has two clauses: every tile value 0..8 appears in exactly one cell, and the blank position points at the unique cell holding 0. The first conjunct is the bijection property -- it is inductive because Slide is a transposition of two cell values and a transposition of a bijection is again a bijection. The second conjunct keeps the blank pointer in sync with the grid, which Slide also preserves by updating both fields together. Bounded state follows from the finite 3x3 grid and finite tile range.
FloodingConsensus: Synchronous flooding consensus tolerates up to f crash failures by running f+1 rounds in which every alive node merges every other alive node's known set into its own. After f+1 rounds, the known sets have converged because at least one round must have occurred without any new crash, and that crash-free round is enough for full information exchange. The spec models per-node known sets, an alive vector, a global round counter, and a per-node decision. Flood is the synchronous round action: it atomically updates every alive node's known set to the union of all alive nodes' sets, then advances the round. Decide(n) fires only at MaxRound and only for an alive, undecided node, which then commits to Min(known[n]) -- using the minimum is the standard tiebreaker that makes agreement deterministic. Crash(n) is bounded by F so the model never violates its assumption. SafetyInv -- two alive deciders agree -- is inductive because Flood synchronizes the known sets across all alive nodes, so by MaxRound any two alive nodes have identical known sets and therefore identical minima.
FreeList: A free-list allocator partitions a fixed pool of blocks into two disjoint sets at every moment: free and used. The spec realizes this directly with two set-valued variables freeBlocks and usedBlocks, and elevates the partition property -- their union is exactly Blocks and their intersection is empty -- to a strong invariant rather than a derived fact. Malloc picks any free block and atomically moves it from freeBlocks to usedBlocks; FreeBlock does the inverse. There is no dynamic state per block beyond which set it is in, because that membership is the entire allocator state. SizeBound asserts the conservation law that |free| + |used| = NumBlocks at all times. Both partition conjuncts and the size bound are inductive because each action removes from one set and adds to the other in the same step, so the union, intersection, and total cardinality are all preserved. The state space is the powerset of Blocks, finite for any concrete NumBlocks.
FsmDoor: A door FSM cycles through closed -> opening -> open -> closing -> closed, and the interesting safety property is not just "no illegal transition" but a constraint on the prior state of certain transitions: opening must follow closed, closing must follow open. To make that a state predicate the spec records prev_state alongside state, updating prev_state on every transition to hold the value of state just before the transition. SafetyInvariant then directly asserts state = opening implies prev_state = closed and state = closing implies prev_state = open, and additionally that opening and prev=closing cannot coexist (which would imply skipping the open phase). cycles is a bounded counter so the model terminates after MaxCycles complete cycles. Each transition action -- StartOpen, FullyOpen, StartClose, FullyClosed -- guards on the current state and writes both state' and prev_state' to the right values, so SafetyInvariant is inductive: every action that produces a new state also writes the matching prev_state.
FsmMicrowave: A microwave with door interlock is a safety-critical FSM whose key invariant is that the heating element only runs when the door is sensed closed. The spec splits the system into four mode states (idle/cooking/paused/finished), a door_closed boolean, a cook_time counter, and an ever_cooked latch. StartCook is guarded on door_closed, which establishes the invariant at every entry into cooking; CookTick advances the timer; Pause and Resume model interruption, with Resume requiring door_closed again so the interlock holds even after a pause. OpenDoor is only enabled in idle or paused, which structurally prevents the door from opening mid-cook -- this is the door interlock mechanically enforced at the action level. CloseDoor restores door_closed to true. Finish moves to finished only when cook_time has reached MaxCookTicks. SafetyInvariant asserts cooking implies door_closed and finished implies cook_time > 0 with ever_cooked, both of which are inductive: cooking can only be entered through StartCook or Resume, and both demand door_closed; finished can only be entered through Finish, which requires cook_time = MaxCookTicks > 0.
FsmTrafficLight: A three-color traffic light is the smallest FSM where strict cycle ordering matters: red -> green -> yellow -> red, with no phase ever skipped or repeated. The spec records both the current color and the previous color so that the cycle invariant -- yellow only follows green, green only follows red -- is a state predicate rather than a temporal one. ToGreen, ToYellow, and ToRed each guard on the current color and write both color' and prev_color' to enforce the cycle. The conjunct ~(color = green /\ prev_color = green) is a sanity check that the same phase isn't entered twice in a row -- a stutter that would otherwise pass the per-edge guards if the actions were misapplied. The ticks counter is a bounded clock to keep the state space finite and to provide the Done action for termination. Inductiveness of SafetyInvariant follows from each transition action writing both fields atomically, so prev_color always equals the predecessor of color in the cycle.
Futex: A futex is a kernel-assisted compare-and-wait primitive whose user-space side is a memory word and whose kernel side is a wait queue keyed on that word's address. The spec abstracts the word as a small natural number and the wait queue as a set of sleeping processes. WaitOn(p) is the atomic compare-and-sleep: it requires word = 0 (the canonical "no event pending" value), checks that p isn't already queued, and adds p to sleepers. SetWord and ClearWord let any thread perturb the word, modeling user-space producers that signal events. WakeOne models wake(1) by removing exactly one process from the sleeper set. The safety predicate FutexSafe constrains |sleepers| <= |Procs| -- a sanity bound that no process is queued twice -- and links sleepers # {} with the word being in its valid range. The atomicity of WaitOn (read word and enqueue in one action) is the property that real futex syscalls implement with kernel-side locking; here it's expressed by the single action's conjunction.
GoBackN: Go-back-N's contract is that the receiver delivers frames in strict sequence and the sender retransmits the entire window on timeout. Five state pieces are needed: base (oldest unacked seq), next (next seq to send), expected (next seq the receiver wants), channel (the in-flight set), and delivered (the actual delivery sequence). The window size bound next - base <= W is one of the load-bearing invariants. SendNew fires only when next - base < W, atomically adding next to channel and bumping next; RetransmitWindow re-injects every seq in [base, next) into channel without changing the counters, modeling the timeout's all-frames-resend behavior. DropFrame removes any in-flight frame, modeling lossy channels. DeliverInOrder is the receiver's strict in-order check: it fires only when expected itself is in channel, then appends expected to delivered and bumps expected. SlideBase walks base toward expected one step at a time, modeling cumulative ack. The TypeOK conjuncts Len(delivered) = expected and delivered[i] = i - 1 collapse the safety claim into a single equation: the delivered prefix is exactly 0,1,...,expected-1, which is inductive because DeliverInOrder is the only action that grows delivered and it grows it by exactly the integer expected which then increments.
